item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Link sort of broken.

Image is by Jessie Eastland, under CC BY-SA 3.0.

item.title: Re: May I ask
item.content:encoded:
Timewatcher, it's generally a good idea to send a wikidot PM to the author of a work if you want to reference their writing in your own material. There's no guarantee they watch the discussion threads of their pages closely.

i wanted to ask if I could use 2037 as one of its pray please ?

Why 2037 in particular? Why not any other prey, even non-anomalous? Might want to give some reasoning for the request when you make it, so it doesn't seem like a casual random thought sort of approach. Authors tend to respond more positively if that sort of thought is put in.

item.title: May I ask
item.content:encoded: Hello I am working on a carnivore scp and i wanted to ask if I could use 2037 as one of its pray please ? I will Let you see the first draft if you want

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea here, and I was entertained by the idea of literal dust bunnies, but the test logs were uninteresting and near the end the structure of the writing felt very awkward. Stuff like "and back to skeleton form █ minutes later" sounds awkward and most of the sentences or smaller bits like this could be cut entirely without losing anything. There's basically a lot of fat here on an otherwise interesting article that really drags it down. No vote.

item.title: Hmm.
item.content:encoded:
Not sure why there's such strong negativity in the comments. What's wrong with simple? The complex scips and simple ones have always seemed to offset each other well. Diversity makes the world go 'round and all that.

The only pokes I can take at 2037 are yes, it does lack some of the higher level of clinical tone that I feel helps build tension and/or mystery in scips (don't add much, I'd say, but scientific document it up a little) and the fact that the bunnies are perfectly susceptible to disease should be emphasized a bit more. If you only add a line right after the cleaning process that references the papilloma addendum, that might ease everyone's concerns about cleaning seeming excessive.

All in all, straightforward, weird, bunnies, still interesting even though its not threatening (gasp!), + 1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry I cant say anything more but: I love the idea, and I love the way its presented. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upboated at "Any sightings of suspicious dust clumps around the facility are to be collected and observed if possible."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. 2037 is probably no longer cursed. Good job.
  2. I was gonna use 2037. Damn you.
  3. I'm not digging this SCP, myself. It's just a punny, if interesting, thing what does a thing. What little narrative there is here doesn't interest me, and the thing's abilities are still not enough to warrant an upvote, no matter how amusing. Sorry. There were also some breaks in clinical tone, but I think the writing itself and the obvious effort don't warrant a downvote, though, so you're getting a novote from me, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oops! I did not mean to type a 10m tall chamber, but rather remaining 3m. A glitch on my phone involving a disappearing keypad is seriously preventing from making edits.

Rabbit cages? Well, although very unlikely, but is it possible for them to escape between bars by entering dust state. Plus the Foundation researchers will probably want to observe them in action, and a cage is not exactly a place you can be active in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now this has gone dumber.
Why have a 10m tall chamber? Like, you realize they don't have to be cube shaped? Besides, I suspect that since you can't have them in an outdoors enclosure where space would not be a problem (Transformation + wind = containment breach) , separating them into groups of 4-5 and holding them inside stacked rabbit cages of appropriate size would be the most sensible option.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and what's with that huge containment enclosure with a three metre roof, to boot?

Um, that was for all 23 specimens. After reading this, I realized that 3m x 3m x 3m is too small for 23 rabbits, which are rather active animals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Um, ok. A lot of thinking.

Also,

it can only transform into a dead rabbit once it dies? What does it accomplish by this

I believe so that I originally intended the dust to be desiring to become animate, but unable to transform into anything alive after its rabbit form died. It's living animate form is dead, so therefore it is completely dead.

Still, I do like the idea of the struggle between the dust and rabbit instincts. Never thought of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You say "something not alive" which clearly has a degree, however small, of agency to change.

Perhaps better wording would be "something not conventionally alive."

Just a thought. I like the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I despise puns. "Dust bunnies" is what pushed me from neutral to negative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tone issues aside, I like the final twist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read it, and probably would have downvoted for that on its own. I intensely dislike having in-universe researchers pontificate in a clinical document, and I especially dislike it when it's used to directly tell the reader what's going on in the article. In about 99% of cases, I find it to be lazy storytelling. And beyond that, why exactly is the idea that it's "dust that turns into rabbits sometimes" so much more compelling than "rabbits that turn into dust sometimes"?

In any event, this article is a D&D monster manual entry predicated on a joke, with filler logs that don't really tell us anything we wouldn't immediately know from the main article description and a completely immersion-breaking "Foundation agent happens to spot anomaly out in the wild at random" recovery entry.

Parenthetically to all of this, I also can't say I'm a fan of "well, you must not be getting it then" as an authorial reaction to criticism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh, yes I did?
While it has a grain of a good idea there, ultimately it's not enough for me to look at this in a positive light.

I wonder though - it can only transform into a dead rabbit once it dies? What does it accomplish by this - certainly not making more of themselves since that's what living matter does.

Unless…
This is rabbit refuse that has, as its preferred state, being a dead rabbit. To this extent it transforms into a live rabbit, who then seeks to die, after fulfilling its basic biologic needs, perhaps including reproduction.

This is the real reason they haven't overpopulated - their rabbit-form's selfpreservation instinct is countered by the inanimate's form desire to become a dead rabbit, which leads to periods of erratic behaviour where they seek death.

So why do they escape from predators? Because they do not want to become eaten rabbits. Just a natural, dessicated corpse. Something like those walled-in buddhist monk mummies.

Who knows, there actually might be a core of a good idea here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You mean the last note that explicitly states they're actually dust clumps that prefer to stay as rabbits when needed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Judging from all the posts in this thread, I am tempted to ask if anyone understood/even read the note in the last addendum?

item.title: Image Source
item.content:encoded:
The image was from http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desert_cottontail#mediaviewer/File:15_for_Dinner.jpg by Jessie Eastland, released under the CC BY-SA 3.0.

(I'm not sure that is exactly how you cite, but it's an attempt)

E: Aaand sorry for late post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, 2037 may not be cursed anymore!

This made me smirk when I realized they're dust bunnies. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While that sounds sensible, the containment procedures should have had some provisions for making this easy, such as, just for an idea a two-section enclosure where you can just alternate having them on either side whenever it's to be cleaned, given the inherent problematic nature of, well, capturing them.

Alternately, startle them and use a giant vacuum cleaner

I also maintain that daily cleaning is an overkill, especially given the enclosure size.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A lot of the wording sounds non-clinical. Other than that, it's just some bunnies that clump up into a dust ball when threatened. Nothing spectacular. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dust bunnies as a scip. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The article didn't particularly interest me but I'm refraining from a downvote because it's literal dust bunnies
Also am I the only one who finds it funny that this and Fire Rabbits are adjacent on the main list?
BUNNIES

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Removal to an adjacent holding pen during cleaning is fairly standard for animal containment. If they aren't particularly intelligent it would be pretty easy to corral them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First thing that popped into my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Literal dust bunnies leave me unimpressed (I'd have expected a little more exploration of the concept - given they can easily escape natural predators, what exactly has been limiting their population up to now? Myxomatosis?) … and what's with that huge containment enclosure with a three metre roof, to boot?

In addition to being inherently a stupid waste of space, in this specific case it makes the personnel's work harder since the containment procedure also involves the removal of all the rabbits from the containment chamber, daily. (Because, guess what, a rabbit will see you catching it or trying to as a 'possible threat' most of the time) I completely do not envy the personnel in charge of that, never mind, it seems like a pointless provision, as ultimately is the daily cleaning.

-1, which is a shame since anomalous biology is one of my sweet spots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't believe I missed that.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dust bunnies? Really? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was just about to say that this was repost of the Fire Rabbits, until I saw the Series III page. Still, I upvoted anyway.
